query: Angular pipes async pipe pure vs impure interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No single-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this exact Angular prompt; the available Angular basics coverage is broader than the question-specific comparison needed here.

competitors.label: Angular Pipes Guide
competitors.selectionReason: The official pipes guide is the canonical source for built-in pipes, async pipe behavior, and pure versus impure pipe semantics.
competitors.winReason: Angular.dev is authoritative on pipes, but our page is more interview-useful because it now pairs the pure-pipe mutation miss with the async-pipe-versus-manual-subscribe decision in one compact answer.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on built-in pipes, custom pipes, async pipe behavior, and pure versus impure semantics.
theirGaps: It is less tightly framed around the real production pitfalls of mutation-in-place and template performance hotspots.

realWorldUseCases: async subscribes/unsubscribes automatically, avoids manual subscription leaks, and marks the view for update when a new value arrives
actionableExamples: this.items.push(newItem); // same array reference => pure pipe may not rerun
letting the async pipe manage subscription and teardown in the template.

realWorldUseCases: The official guide explains pipes thoroughly, but our page is more direct about the specific production bugs around leaks, OnPush, and mutation traps.
actionableExamples: Angular.dev provides strong pipe examples, but our page now pairs the pure-pipe mutation miss with the async-pipe-versus-manual-subscribe contrast in one compact worked block.
followUpConfusion: It covers the concepts well, but our page is a little more direct on why pure pipes miss in-place mutations.
Keep one short line on when expensive transforms should move out of template pipes and into selectors or computed state.

competitors.label: Telerik Pure vs Impure Pipes
competitors.selectionReason: This is a direct tutorial competitor for the pure-versus-impure performance trade-off and pipe behavior.
competitors.winReason: Telerik is strong on pure-versus-impure behavior, but our page now wins the broader prompt by pairing mutation traps with async-pipe usage and a clearer rule for moving heavy work out of the template.
theirStrengths: It is tightly focused on the exact pure-versus-impure distinction and the performance consequences.
theirGaps: It is narrower on async pipe and the interview-grade advice about keeping pipes deterministic and side-effect free.

actionableExamples: this.items.push(newItem); // same array reference => pure pipe may not rerun
letting the async pipe manage subscription and teardown in the template.
followUpConfusion: If the transform is heavy, list-sized, or needs memoization keys, move it out of the template into a selector, computed signal, facade, or RxJS pipeline instead of reaching for an impure pipe.

realWorldUseCases: Telerik keeps the page centered on real performance consequences of pure versus impure pipes, so the use-case framing is close.
actionableExamples: Telerik is strong on pure versus impure behavior, but our page now gives the stronger compact pair: pure-pipe mutation miss plus async-pipe-versus-manual-subscribe.
followUpConfusion: Our page is now more rounded on the actual decision boundary: async pipe for rendering streams, pure pipes for cheap transforms, and selectors or computed state for heavy work.

realWorldUseCases: Keep the answer anchored on async pipe plus mutation traps rather than letting it drift into formatting-only examples.
